# Transformers, Self-Attention, and Prompt Engineering

At the core of the transformer architecture lies the self-attention mechanism. Self-attention
turns each token representation into a contextual representation by letting that token compare
itself with every other token in the sequence. Raschka's from-scratch walkthrough is especially
useful because it shows the computation one tensor at a time: embeddings become queries, keys,
and values; query-key dot products become attention weights; attention weights mix value vectors
into context vectors [@raschka2023selfattentionfromscratch].

The original Transformer has two major stacks: an encoder stack and a decoder stack. Modern
models often use only part of that design. BERT is encoder-only, GPT is decoder-only, and T5/BART
use an encoder-decoder stack. The encoder discerns relationships between positions in the input
sequence, while decoder-style models use masked self-attention so generation cannot inspect future
tokens. To retain sequence order, the model adopts positional encoding, ensuring it preserves the
initial order of data. Multi-head attention allows the model to channel attention toward multiple
data points simultaneously, capturing a wider range of information from the same input words. This
architecture powers the prompt-based generative tasks we rely on today.

## Self-Attention From Scratch

Start with a tokenized sentence and an embedding matrix:

$$
X \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times d}
$$

where $T$ is the sequence length and $d$ is the embedding dimension. A single attention head
uses three learned projection matrices:

$$
W_Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \qquad
W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \qquad
W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}.
$$

These produce:

$$
Q=XW_Q,\qquad K=XW_K,\qquad V=XW_V.
$$

For one token $i$, the model compares its query vector $q_i$ with every key vector $k_j$:

$$
\omega_{ij}=q_i k_j^\top.
$$

The raw scores are divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ and normalized across the row:

$$
\alpha_{ij} =
\frac{\exp(\omega_{ij}/\sqrt{d_k})}
{\sum_{\ell=1}^{T}\exp(\omega_{i\ell}/\sqrt{d_k})}.
$$

Then the contextual vector for token $i$ is a weighted sum of value vectors:

$$
z_i=\sum_{j=1}^{T}\alpha_{ij}v_j.
$$

For all tokens at once, this is the familiar matrix expression:

$$
Z=\mathrm{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V.
$$

Raschka's explanation emphasizes a helpful interpretation: each row of the attention matrix is
one token's reading plan over the sequence. The token does not simply keep its original embedding;
it receives a context vector assembled from other token value vectors.

::: {#75a1e067 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

torch.manual_seed(123)
tokens = torch.tensor([0, 4, 5, 2, 1, 3])
embedding = torch.nn.Embedding(num_embeddings=6, embedding_dim=16)
X = embedding(tokens)

d_model = X.shape[-1]
d_k, d_v = 24, 28
W_Q = torch.randn(d_model, d_k)
W_K = torch.randn(d_model, d_k)
W_V = torch.randn(d_model, d_v)

Q = X @ W_Q
K = X @ W_K
V = X @ W_V

scores = Q @ K.T
attention = F.softmax(scores / d_k**0.5, dim=-1)
Z = attention @ V

print("X:", X.shape)
print("Q/K/V:", Q.shape, K.shape, V.shape)
print("attention:", attention.shape)
print("context vectors:", Z.shape)
print("row sums:", attention.sum(dim=-1))
```
:::


## Causal Masking for GPT-Style Models

The formula above is bidirectional: every token can read every other token. GPT-style decoders
use the same self-attention machinery, but apply a causal mask before softmax. Scores for future
positions are replaced with a very negative number, so softmax assigns those positions probability
near zero.

::: {#000409ea .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
T = scores.shape[0]
causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=1)
masked_scores = scores.masked_fill(causal_mask, float("-inf"))
causal_attention = F.softmax(masked_scores / d_k**0.5, dim=-1)

print(causal_attention.round(decimals=3))
```
:::


This is the mathematical reason a decoder can be trained on full sequences while still learning
the left-to-right behavior it must use at generation time.

## Encoder, Decoder, and Encoder-Decoder Stacks

It is useful to keep two explanations in mind: the technical stack and the modeling behavior.

| Model family | Stack | Attention pattern | What the model is naturally good at |
|---|---|---|---|
| BERT | Encoder-only | Bidirectional self-attention | Understanding a complete input |
| GPT | Decoder-only | Causal masked self-attention | Predicting the next token |
| T5/BART | Encoder-decoder | Encoder self-attention, decoder self-attention, decoder cross-attention | Mapping one sequence to another |

Technically, an encoder block contains bidirectional self-attention followed by a feed-forward
network. A GPT-style decoder-only block contains causal self-attention followed by a feed-forward
network. The original Transformer decoder, and encoder-decoder models such as T5, add one more
attention sublayer: cross-attention, where decoder queries attend to encoder keys and values.

```text
BERT-style encoder block
  hidden states
    -> bidirectional self-attention
    -> feed-forward network

GPT-style decoder-only block
  hidden states
    -> causal self-attention
    -> feed-forward network

Encoder-decoder decoder block
  target hidden states
    -> causal self-attention
    -> cross-attention over encoder states
    -> feed-forward network
```

Explanatorily, BERT reads the whole sentence before making a representation. That is why it is
strong for classification, named entity recognition, semantic search, and extractive tasks. GPT
reads only the prefix available so far. That is why it is strong for generation, chat, and
completion. Encoder-decoder models first encode a source sequence, then decode a target sequence
conditioned on that source, which fits translation and summarization.



# From Pretraining to Prompting {#sec-intro}

In Module 4 Lecture 1 we saw how transformers are *pretrained*: masked language modeling
(BERT), causal language modeling (GPT), and span corruption (T5) teach a model to represent
language deeply from raw text. Pretraining produces a powerful general-purpose encoder or
decoder — but a general-purpose model still needs to be aimed at a specific task.

This lecture covers the full spectrum of **adaptation strategies**:

| Strategy | Cost | Data needed | Suited for |
|----------|------|-------------|------------|
| Prompt engineering | Zero compute | Zero labels | Rapid prototyping, task spec |
| Few-shot prompting | Zero compute | 3–10 examples | Low-data settings |
| Instruction tuning | Medium | 10k–1M examples | Multi-task generalization |
| RLHF / DPO | High | Human preference pairs | Alignment, safety |
| Full fine-tuning | High | 1k–100k labels | Max accuracy, domain shift |
| LoRA / Adapters | Low | 1k–100k labels | VRAM-constrained adaptation |


# The Shift to Prompt-Based Approaches

## From Training-Centric to Inference-Centric AI

For most of ML history, behavior was determined at **training**. Once deployed, a model was frozen: changing behavior required retraining. LLMs shatter this assumption. Their behavior is no longer fixed at deployment — it can be **steered at inference time** through prompts, context, and task specifications.

This is the **Pre-train, Prompt, and Predict** paradigm [@liu2023pretrain]:

- **Pretrain**: learn a general language model on a massive unlabelled corpus.
- **Prompt**: supply a natural-language template that converts the downstream task into a language modeling problem.
- **Predict**: let the model fill in the blanks — no gradient updates required.

## Formal Notation

Given:
- $x$ — original input (e.g., a sentence)
- $f_{\text{prompt}}(x)$ — a **prompting function** that converts $x$ into a prompted input $x'$ (fills in a template around $x$)
- $x'$ — the prompted input (template + input)
- $z$ — the **answer** (filled slot, e.g., a class label, a word, or a generated span)
- $f_{\text{fill}}(x', z)$ — the **answer search** function that scores candidate answers $z$

The prediction is:

$$
\begin{align}
\hat{z} = \arg\max_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \, P(f_{\text{fill}}(x', z) \mid \theta)
\end{align}
$$

where $\mathcal{Z}$ is the set of candidate answers and $\theta$ are the (frozen) model parameters.

**Example** — sentiment classification:

| Step | Text |
|------|------|
| Input $x$ | "The quarterly earnings exceeded expectations." |
| Template | "[X] The sentiment of this text is [Z]." |
| Prompted $x'$ | "The quarterly earnings exceeded expectations. The sentiment of this text is ___" |
| Answer space $\mathcal{Z}$ | {"positive", "negative", "neutral"} |
| Prediction $\hat{z}$ | "positive" (highest log-prob under the LM) |


## Classical Learning vs. Generative Conditioning

### Classical Supervised Learning

In the classical paradigm:

$$
\begin{align}
y = f_\theta(x)
\end{align}
$$

$\theta$ encodes all learned behavior. Changing the model's behavior requires modifying $\theta$.
This creates a rigid workflow: expensive offline training followed by static, brittle deployment.

### Generative Conditioning

Generative models define a conditional distribution:

$$
\begin{align}
P(Y \mid X, \theta)
\end{align}
$$

Adding a prompt $p$ at inference time adds a third control variable:

$$
\begin{align}
P(Y \mid X, p, \theta)
\end{align}
$$

Prompts act as *soft constraints*: they reshape the model's posterior distribution without touching the weights.


## Prompt as an Inference-Time Control Signal

A prompt functions as a **control interface** for a probabilistic model:

- **Conditioning signal** — narrows the space of plausible outputs (Bayesian analogy).
- **Soft constraint** — biases trajectories probabilistically, not deterministically.
- **Semantic policy** — encodes how the model should behave ("Be concise", "Act as a SQL expert").
- **Steering mechanism** — alters token likelihoods to enable rapid task adaptation without retraining.

```{dot}
digraph InferenceConditioning {
    rankdir=TB;
    node [shape=rectangle, style=rounded, fontsize=12];
    subgraph cluster_input {
        label="Inputs";
        style=dashed;
        X [label="User Input\nX"];
        P [label="Prompt / Context\np"];
    }
    Theta [label="Model Parameters\nθ (frozen)", shape=rectangle, style="rounded,filled", fillcolor=lightgray];
    subgraph cluster_inference {
        label="Inference Process";
        style=dashed;
        Combine [label="Conditioning:\n[X, p]"];
        LM [label="Generative Model\nP(Y | X, p, θ)"];
    }
    Y [label="Output Distribution\nY"];
    X -> Combine;
    P -> Combine;
    Theta -> LM;
    Combine -> LM;
    LM -> Y;
}
```


## Prompt Engineering, Fine-Tuning, and RAG: A Comparative View

![Venn Diagram of Prompt Engineering, Fine-tuning, and RAG](../M3/M03_lecture01_figures/Prompt_Engineering_vs_RAG-tuning_Venn_Diagram.jpg){width=60% fig-align=center fig-alt="Venn diagram showing the relationship between prompt engineering, fine-tuning, and RAG" #fig-venn-diagram}

| Approach | Weight Update | Data Required | Iteration Speed | Best For |
|----------|:---:|---|---|---|
| **Prompt Engineering** | None | 0 labels | Minutes | Task spec, style control |
| **Fine-Tuning** | Yes — all | Labeled examples | Hours–days | Domain adaptation, new skills |
| **RAG** | None | Document corpus | Minutes | Factual accuracy, recency |
| **LoRA / PEFT** | Yes — subset | Labeled examples | Hours | VRAM-limited adaptation |

They interact: **Prompting + RAG** = reasoning over external knowledge; **Prompting + Fine-tuning** = stable specialized behavior; **RAG + Fine-tuning** = domain-specific grounding.


## Worked Example: How a Prompt Reshapes Token Probabilities

### Without a Prompt

Input: `"Complete the sentence: The cat"`

The model's next-token distribution (pretrained priors):

| Token | Probability |
|:-------|:-------------:|
| runs  | 0.40 |
| sleeps | 0.35 |
| . | 0.15 |
| dog | 0.05 |
| cat | 0.05 |

### With a Dramatic-Scene Prompt

Prompt: `"Write a dramatic action scene. Complete the sentence: The cat"`

| Token | Probability |
|:-------|:-------------:|
| runs | 0.55 |
| leaps | 0.25 |
| attacks | 0.10 |
| sleeps | 0.03 |
| . | 0.02 |

"sleeps" collapses 0.35 → 0.03. No weights changed — only the **conditioning context**.

### With a Role + Style Prompt

Prompt: `"You are a veterinarian writing a calm, factual report. Complete the sentence: The cat"`

| Token | Probability |
|:-------|:-------------:|
| sleeps | 0.50 |
| rests | 0.20 |
| is | 0.15 |
| runs | 0.05 |
| . | 0.05 |

The prompt now acts as a **semantic policy** — "calm, factual" shifts the distribution toward clinical, low-emotion verbs.


## Training Settings: Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, Full-Data

The prompting framework naturally accommodates different data regimes [@liu2023pretrain]:

| Setting | Labeled examples | Parameter update? | Notes |
|---------|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Zero-shot** | 0 | No | Model infers task from the template alone |
| **Few-shot (in-context)** | 1–10 | No | Examples in the prompt context window |
| **Full-data supervised** | 1k–100k | Yes (fine-tune) | Standard supervised learning on top of pretrained features |

In-context few-shot is a distinguishing *emergent ability* of large models: GPT-3 (175B) can solve new tasks from 3 examples; GPT-2 (1.5B) largely cannot.

## Tiny Adaptation Examples

The adaptation spectrum becomes easier to see with short business sentences:

| Strategy | Tiny input | Desired behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Prompting | "Summarize quarterly risks in two bullets." | Follow the requested format without training. |
| Few-shot prompting | "Revenue rose despite higher cloud costs." | Match the labeling pattern shown in context. |
| Fine-tuning | "Classify vendor delays as supply-chain risk." | Learn a stable task-specific label mapping. |

In all three cases, we are using the same broad transformer family. The exact stack may differ:
BERT-style systems usually adapt an encoder representation, while GPT-style systems adapt the
decoder-only next-token distribution. What changes within a stack is the control signal: the
prompt, the in-context examples, or a weight update.

![BERT-style fine-tuning adds a task head on top of pretrained representations.](./M04_lecture02_figures/bert_finetune_classification.svg){width=70% fig-align=center fig-alt="BERT encoder with a classification head for fine-tuning."}

## Section 4.3: GPT, Causal Context, and Prompting

The GPT notebook is the executable companion for this lecture. It starts from the same scaled dot-product attention used in the transformer architecture notebook, then adds a causal mask so each token can only attend to previous tokens. That one constraint turns the transformer decoder into an autoregressive generator.

Short generation example:

| Step | Visible context | Next-token target |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | "The analyst expects" | `revenue` |
| 2 | "The analyst expects revenue" | `growth` |
| 3 | "The analyst expects revenue growth" | `this` |

This left-to-right training objective is why a decoder-only model can generate fluent text from a prompt. It is also why prompt wording matters: every token in the prompt becomes part of the visible causal context.

![GPT is the decoder-only branch of the transformer family.](./M04_lecture02_figures/elmo_gpt_bert.svg){width=70% fig-align=center fig-alt="Comparison of ELMo, GPT, and BERT architectures."}


# Prompting Patterns: Code Demonstrations

## Zero-Shot vs Few-Shot Prompt Construction

These cells demonstrate prompt formatting patterns in pure Python — no API key required.
The patterns translate directly to any LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, HuggingFace).

::: {#8d743914 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def zero_shot_prompt(task: str, input_text: str) -> str:
    return f"{task}\n\nInput: {input_text}\nOutput:"

def few_shot_prompt(task: str, examples: list[dict], input_text: str) -> str:
    prompt = task + "\n\n"
    for ex in examples:
        prompt += f"Input: {ex['input']}\nOutput: {ex['output']}\n\n"
    prompt += f"Input: {input_text}\nOutput:"
    return prompt

task = "Classify the sentiment of the following text as POSITIVE, NEGATIVE, or NEUTRAL."
examples = [
    {"input": "The product exceeded all my expectations.",  "output": "POSITIVE"},
    {"input": "Delivery was delayed by two weeks.",          "output": "NEGATIVE"},
    {"input": "The report was submitted on time.",           "output": "NEUTRAL"},
]
test_text = "Revenue grew 12% year-over-year driven by strong cloud adoption."

print("=== ZERO-SHOT PROMPT ===")
print(zero_shot_prompt(task, test_text))
print()
print("=== FEW-SHOT PROMPT (k=3) ===")
print(few_shot_prompt(task, examples, test_text))
```
:::


## Chain-of-Thought Prompting

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting instructs the model to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer. It is especially effective for multi-step problems.

::: {#33e65cfa .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def cot_prompt(task: str, examples: list[dict], input_text: str) -> str:
    prompt = task + "\n\n"
    for ex in examples:
        prompt += f"Question: {ex['question']}\n"
        prompt += f"Reasoning: {ex['reasoning']}\n"
        prompt += f"Answer: {ex['answer']}\n\n"
    prompt += f"Question: {input_text}\nReasoning:"
    return prompt

task_cot = (
    "You are a financial analyst. "
    "For each question, show your reasoning step by step, "
    "then provide a concise final answer."
)
cot_examples = [
    {
        "question": "A company reports $500M revenue with 20% YoY growth. What was last year's revenue?",
        "reasoning": "last_year × 1.20 = $500M → last_year = $500M / 1.20 = $416.7M",
        "answer": "Last year's revenue was approximately $416.7 million.",
    },
    {
        "question": "Operating margin is 15% on $200M revenue. What is operating income?",
        "reasoning": "Operating income = $200M × 0.15 = $30M.",
        "answer": "Operating income is $30 million.",
    },
]
question = "A firm's net income grew from $80M to $104M. What is the percentage growth?"
print(cot_prompt(task_cot, cot_examples, question))
```
:::


## Role Prompting and Structured Output

::: {#2fcfa2d5 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def role_prompt(role: str, context: str, request: str) -> str:
    return (
        f"You are {role}.\n\n"
        f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
        f"Task: {request}\n\n"
        f"Respond in valid JSON."
    )

role = "a senior equity research analyst specializing in technology sector 10-K filings"
context = """
Item 1A. Risk Factors (excerpt):
The Company faces intense competition from well-established and emerging companies.
Rapid technological changes could render our products obsolete.
We are subject to significant regulatory requirements in multiple jurisdictions.
"""
request = (
    "Extract the top 3 risk categories from the excerpt. "
    "For each, provide: category, description (one sentence), and severity (High/Medium/Low)."
)
print(role_prompt(role, context, request))
```
:::


# Full Fine-Tuning

All model parameters are updated during training on labelled task data.

### Classification Head

::: {#b4b4c4ee .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch.nn as nn

class TransformerClassifier(nn.Module):
    """
    Wrap a frozen (or partially frozen) transformer encoder with a classification head.
    In practice, replace `encoder` with a HuggingFace model.
    """
    def __init__(self, encoder: nn.Module, hidden_dim: int, num_classes: int,
                 dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder    = encoder
        self.dropout    = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B, T) token ids → (B, T, H) hidden states
        hidden = self.encoder(x)          # placeholder call
        cls    = hidden[:, 0, :]          # take [CLS] representation
        return self.classifier(self.dropout(cls))
```
:::


### Fine-Tuning Training Loop

::: {#57a1dcbf .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch

def fine_tune(model, train_loader, val_loader, epochs: int = 3, lr: float = 2e-5):
    device    = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    model     = model.to(device)
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=0.01)
    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    for epoch in range(1, epochs + 1):
        model.train()
        total_loss = correct = total = 0
        for x, y in train_loader:
            x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            logits = model(x)
            loss   = criterion(logits, y)
            loss.backward()
            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
            optimizer.step()
            total_loss += loss.item()
            correct    += (logits.argmax(-1) == y).sum().item()
            total      += len(y)
        train_acc = correct / total

        model.eval()
        correct = total = 0
        with torch.no_grad():
            for x, y in val_loader:
                x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
                correct += (model(x).argmax(-1) == y).sum().item()
                total   += len(y)
        print(f"Epoch {epoch}  loss={total_loss/len(train_loader):.4f}  "
              f"train_acc={train_acc:.3f}  val_acc={correct/total:.3f}")
```
:::


**Practical tips**: Use a small learning rate (1e-5 – 5e-5), warm up the learning rate for
the first 6–10% of steps, and apply weight decay to all parameters except biases and
LayerNorm weights.

## Instruction Tuning

Instruction tuning fine-tunes a model on **(instruction, response)** pairs drawn from a wide
variety of tasks, teaching the model to follow natural language commands zero-shot.

### Prompt Format

::: {#b231c0db .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def format_instruction_prompt(instruction: str, input_text: str = "",
                               response: str = "") -> str:
    parts = [f"### Instruction:\n{instruction}"]
    if input_text:
        parts.append(f"### Input:\n{input_text}")
    parts.append(f"### Response:\n{response}")
    return "\n\n".join(parts)


example = format_instruction_prompt(
    instruction="Classify the sentiment of the following financial headline.",
    input_text="Apple reports record-breaking Q4 revenue.",
    response="POSITIVE",
)
print(example)
```
:::


Key datasets: FLAN (1,800 tasks), Alpaca (52k GPT-4-generated examples), Dolly, OpenAssistant.

## RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback

RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022) aligns a language model with human preferences through three stages:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**: Fine-tune on demonstrations of the desired behaviour.
2. **Reward Model**: Train a model to predict human preference scores from ranked output pairs.
3. **PPO Optimisation**: Use Proximal Policy Optimization to maximise reward while a KL
   penalty keeps the policy close to the SFT baseline.

$$\mathcal{L}_\text{PPO} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[r(x,y) - \beta \cdot D_\text{KL}(\pi_\theta \,\|\, \pi_\text{SFT})\right]$$

**Alternative — DPO** (Rafailov et al., 2023): directly optimises a preference objective
without a separate reward model, dramatically simplifying the training pipeline.

## Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)

### The Problem

Full fine-tuning updates every model parameter. That is simple conceptually, but expensive in
three separate ways:

1. **Training memory**: the GPU must hold weights, gradients, optimizer states, activations,
   and temporary buffers. Adam-style optimizer states alone can multiply memory needs.
2. **Storage**: each task produces a full copy of the model checkpoint.
3. **Operations**: moving, validating, and serving many full checkpoints becomes a platform
   problem rather than a modeling problem.

PEFT methods freeze the pretrained weights and add a small number of trainable parameters.
This works because downstream adaptation is often lower-dimensional than the full parameter
space. Aghajanyan et al. show that fine-tuning can be effective in a much smaller intrinsic
dimension than the raw parameter count would suggest [@Aghajanyan2020IntrinsicDimensionality].
LoRA turns that empirical insight into a practical adapter method [@Hu2021LoRA].

### Full Fine-Tuning vs. PEFT

| Question | Full fine-tuning | PEFT / LoRA |
|---|---|---|
| What changes? | All model weights | Small adapter weights |
| What is saved? | Full checkpoint | Adapter checkpoint |
| Training memory | Highest | Much lower |
| Inference path | Native model | Load adapter or merge it |
| Best use | Maximum task accuracy with enough data and compute | Fast domain/task adaptation |

The main tradeoff is control. Full fine-tuning can move the whole model, which may help with
large domain shifts. PEFT is more constrained, but that constraint is exactly why it is cheap,
modular, and less prone to destroying general capabilities.

### LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation

LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices:

$$W' = W + \Delta W = W + B A$$

where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, and $r \ll \min(d, k)$.
The original weight $W$ is frozen. During training, gradients update only $A$ and $B$.

For a square projection matrix with $d=k=4096$, a full update has $4096 \times 4096 =
16,777,216$ trainable values. A rank-8 LoRA update has $8(4096+4096)=65,536$ trainable
values, only 0.39% of the full update.

LoRA is commonly inserted into attention projection layers:

| Module | Role | Common LoRA choice |
|---|---|---|
| `q_proj` | Query projection | Often targeted |
| `k_proj` | Key projection | Optional |
| `v_proj` | Value projection | Often targeted |
| `o_proj` | Output projection | Common for instruction tuning |
| MLP projections | Feed-forward transformations | Useful for larger behavior shifts |

This module list is easier to interpret after the self-attention derivation. A LoRA adapter on
`q_proj` changes how a token asks for information. An adapter on `k_proj` changes what other
tokens look matchable against. An adapter on `v_proj` changes the information that gets blended
into context vectors. An adapter on `o_proj` changes how head outputs are remixed before the next
transformer sublayer. This is why attention projection adapters can strongly shift model behavior
while changing only a small fraction of the full model parameters.

::: {#651a54b6 .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    """Drop-in replacement for nn.Linear with LoRA adaptation."""
    def __init__(self, in_features: int, out_features: int,
                 rank: int = 8, alpha: float = 16.0, dropout: float = 0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.rank    = rank
        self.alpha   = alpha
        self.scaling = alpha / rank

        # Frozen pretrained weight (no gradient)
        self.weight  = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(out_features, in_features), requires_grad=False)
        self.bias    = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(out_features), requires_grad=False)

        # Trainable low-rank matrices
        self.lora_A  = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, in_features)  * (1 / rank)**0.5)
        self.lora_B  = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(out_features, rank))
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        base = F.linear(x, self.weight, self.bias)
        lora = F.linear(self.dropout(x), self.lora_A)   # (B, T, rank)
        lora = F.linear(lora, self.lora_B)               # (B, T, out)
        return base + lora * self.scaling

    def merge_weights(self) -> nn.Linear:
        """Fuse LoRA into the base weight for zero-latency inference."""
        merged = self.weight + (self.lora_B @ self.lora_A) * self.scaling
        layer  = nn.Linear(self.weight.size(1), self.weight.size(0), bias=True)
        layer.weight.data = merged
        layer.bias.data   = self.bias.clone()
        return layer


# Parameter count comparison
d, k, r = 768, 768, 8
full_params = d * k
lora_params = r * (d + k)
print(f"Full weight: {full_params:,} params")
print(f"LoRA (r={r}):  {lora_params:,} params  ({lora_params/full_params*100:.1f}%)")
```
:::


### Hugging Face PEFT Example

In practice, we rarely hand-edit every layer. The `peft` library wraps a Transformers model and
inserts LoRA modules for us.

::: {#2e694280 .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import LoraConfig, TaskType, get_peft_model

model_name = "TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
```
:::


For a finance classification or extraction task, the most important design decision is the text
format. The model should repeatedly see the same structure at training and inference time.

::: {#8a9740e4 .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def format_risk_example(sentence: str, label: str | None = None) -> str:
    response = "" if label is None else label
    return f"""### Instruction:
Classify the disclosure sentence as one risk type:
competition, regulation, supply_chain, macro, cybersecurity, other.

### Input:
{sentence}

### Response:
{response}"""


example = format_risk_example(
    "The Company faces intense competition from established and emerging firms.",
    "competition",
)
print(example)
```
:::


### QLoRA: Quantized LoRA

QLoRA adds one more move: load the frozen base model in 4-bit precision and train LoRA adapters
through it. Dettmers et al. introduce three memory-saving ingredients [@Dettmers2023QLoRA]:

- **NF4 quantization**: a 4-bit NormalFloat data type designed for normally distributed weights.
- **Double quantization**: quantize the constants used by quantization itself.
- **Paged optimizers**: reduce memory spikes during training.

The result is a practical recipe for adapting models that would otherwise be too large for a
single GPU.

::: {#643046c7 .cell execution_count=12}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, TaskType, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0",
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
)
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)

qlora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
)
model = get_peft_model(model, qlora_config)
```
:::


Use QLoRA when the base model does not fit comfortably in 16-bit precision. Use ordinary LoRA
when the model already fits and you want simpler training/debugging.

### LoRA/QLoRA Training Loop with `Trainer`

The following skeleton shows the moving parts. The exact dataset can be the Apple 10-K
classification set in `help-code/4-nlp-finance/data/evaluation/`.

::: {#8e456f83 .cell execution_count=13}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from datasets import Dataset
from transformers import DataCollatorForLanguageModeling, Trainer, TrainingArguments

records = [
    {
        "text": format_risk_example(
            "The Company is subject to complex laws and regulations worldwide.",
            "regulation",
        )
    },
    {
        "text": format_risk_example(
            "Component shortages could delay product shipments.",
            "supply_chain",
        )
    },
]
dataset = Dataset.from_list(records)

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(batch["text"], truncation=True, max_length=512)

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="outputs/risk-lora",
    per_device_train_batch_size=1,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    num_train_epochs=2,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    report_to="none",
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenized,
    data_collator=collator,
)
trainer.train()
model.save_pretrained("outputs/risk-lora-adapter")
```
:::


This is causal-language-model training: the model learns to produce the response tokens after
the prompt. For pure classification, a sequence-classification head can be more efficient, but
the instruction format is useful because it mirrors how chat models are used in business
workflows.

### Practical Hyperparameters

| Setting | Starting point | What it controls |
|---|---:|---|
| LoRA rank `r` | 8 or 16 | Adapter capacity |
| `lora_alpha` | 2x rank | Update scale |
| `lora_dropout` | 0.05 | Regularization |
| Learning rate | `1e-4` to `2e-4` | Speed and stability |
| Epochs | 1-3 | Exposure to small datasets |
| Max sequence length | 512-2048 | Disclosure context retained |
| Gradient accumulation | 4-16 | Effective batch size |

When results are poor, inspect examples before changing rank. In domain fine-tuning, mislabeled
or inconsistent data is usually more damaging than a suboptimal adapter rank.

### Evaluation for Finance Fine-Tuning

Fine-tuning should be evaluated on behavior, not just training loss:

- **Label accuracy/F1** for risk classification.
- **Exact format compliance** for JSON or label-only outputs.
- **Held-out company or held-out section splits** to reduce leakage.
- **Calibration checks** for high-stakes categories such as cybersecurity or regulation.
- **Comparison against prompting/RAG baselines** to prove fine-tuning is worth the added workflow.

Fine-tuning is not a good way to inject fresh facts. If the task requires current filings,
policies, or market information, use retrieval. Fine-tuning is best for stable behavior:
taxonomy mapping, extraction format, tone, routing rules, and task-specific decision boundaries.

### Adapter Modules

Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) insert small feed-forward bottleneck layers *inside* each
transformer block.

::: {#5df95bad .cell execution_count=14}
``` {.python .cell-code}
class AdapterModule(nn.Module):
    """Bottleneck adapter inserted after attention and FFN sub-layers."""
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim: int, bottleneck_dim: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.down   = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, bottleneck_dim)
        self.act    = nn.GELU()
        self.up     = nn.Linear(bottleneck_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.dropout= nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        return x + self.up(self.dropout(self.act(self.down(x))))   # residual


adapter = AdapterModule(hidden_dim=768, bottleneck_dim=64)
x = torch.randn(2, 10, 768)
print(f"Adapter output: {adapter(x).shape}")
total = sum(p.numel() for p in adapter.parameters())
print(f"Trainable params: {total:,}  vs  768×768 = {768*768:,}")
```
:::


## Summary

| Method | Updates | Params (7B) | Use When |
|--------|---------|-------------|----------|
| Prompt engineering | None | 0 MB | Zero-label task spec, rapid prototyping |
| Few-shot (in-context) | None | 0 MB | 3–10 examples, no GPU |
| Full fine-tuning | All | ~14 GB (fp16) | Large data, max accuracy |
| Instruction tuning | All | ~14 GB | Multi-task generalisation |
| LoRA (r=8) | A, B matrices | ~20 MB | Limited VRAM, fast iteration |
| QLoRA | LoRA adapters + 4-bit frozen base | Adapter MBs + quantized base | Larger models on smaller GPUs |
| Adapters | Bottleneck layers | ~50 MB | Modular task plug-ins |
| Prompt tuning | Soft prompt only | ~1 MB | Very limited compute |
